instruction: How do I set up a modern C++ project with CMake presets and FetchContent for dependency management?
output:
<think>
CMake presets (CMakePresets.json) standardize build configurations so everyone on the team uses the same settings. Instead of `cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -G Ninja ..`, you run `cmake --preset release`. This eliminates 'works on my machine' build issues.

FetchContent replaces the old pattern of git submodules or manually downloading dependencies. It downloads and builds dependencies at configure time, integrating them directly into your build. For header-only libraries, it is almost zero overhead.

The key CMake structure: top-level CMakeLists.txt defines the project, sets up FetchContent for deps, then add_subdirectory for your own libraries and executables. Each subdirectory has its own CMakeLists.txt with target_* commands.

Modern CMake is target-based: instead of setting global include directories and link flags, you set them per-target with target_include_directories, target_link_libraries, etc. Dependencies propagate through PUBLIC/PRIVATE/INTERFACE keywords.
</think>

```cpp
// === CMakePresets.json ===
// {
//   "version": 6,
//   "configurePresets": [
//     {
//       "name": "dev",
//       "binaryDir": "build/dev",
//       "generator": "Ninja",
//       "cacheVariables": {
//         "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE": "Debug",
//         "CMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS": "ON",
//         "BUILD_TESTING": "ON"
//       }
//     },
//     {
//       "name": "release",
//       "binaryDir": "build/release",
//       "generator": "Ninja",
//       "cacheVariables": {
//         "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE": "Release",
//         "BUILD_TESTING": "OFF"
//       }
//     }
//   ],
//   "buildPresets": [
//     { "name": "dev", "configurePreset": "dev" },
//     { "name": "release", "configurePreset": "release" }
//   ]
// }

// === CMakeLists.txt (root) ===
// cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.25)
// project(myapp VERSION 1.0.0 LANGUAGES CXX)
// set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
// set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
//
// include(FetchContent)
//
// FetchContent_Declare(
//   fmt
//   GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/fmtlib/fmt.git
//   GIT_TAG 10.2.1
// )
// FetchContent_Declare(
//   googletest
//   GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/google/googletest.git
//   GIT_TAG v1.14.0
// )
// FetchContent_MakeAvailable(fmt)
// if(BUILD_TESTING)
//   FetchContent_MakeAvailable(googletest)
//   enable_testing()
// endif()
//
// add_subdirectory(src)
// if(BUILD_TESTING)
//   add_subdirectory(tests)
// endif()

// === src/CMakeLists.txt ===
// add_library(mylib
//   core/engine.cpp
//   core/parser.cpp
//   utils/string_utils.cpp
// )
// target_include_directories(mylib PUBLIC ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR})
// target_link_libraries(mylib PUBLIC fmt::fmt)
//
// add_executable(myapp main.cpp)
// target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE mylib)

// === src/core/engine.h ===
#pragma once
#include <string>

namespace myapp {
class Engine {
public:
    explicit Engine(std::string config_path);
    std::string process(const std::string& input);
private:
    std::string config_path_;
};
}

// Build:
// cmake --preset dev && cmake --build --preset dev
// cmake --preset release && cmake --build --preset release
// ctest --preset dev  // run tests
```

instruction: How do I create proper install targets and packaging with CMake so others can use my C++ library?
output:
<think>
Making your C++ library installable with CMake means users can find_package(MyLib) and link against it. This requires: (1) install targets for headers and the library, (2) export sets for CMake config files, (3) version config for compatibility checking.

The install(TARGETS) command copies built artifacts. install(DIRECTORY) copies headers. install(EXPORT) generates CMake files that let find_package work. The generated MyLibConfig.cmake and MyLibTargets.cmake files tell CMake where to find your library and headers.

For header-only libraries, you create an INTERFACE library (no compiled code) and install just the headers plus the CMake config.

The key commands: install(TARGETS mylib EXPORT MyLibTargets), install(EXPORT MyLibTargets), configure_package_config_file for version compatibility. After `cmake --install`, users can `find_package(MyLib 1.0 REQUIRED)` and `target_link_libraries(their_app MyLib::mylib)`.
</think>

```cpp
// === CMakeLists.txt (installable library) ===
// cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.25)
// project(MyLib VERSION 1.0.0 LANGUAGES CXX)
// set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
//
// include(GNUInstallDirs)
// include(CMakePackageConfigHelpers)
//
// add_library(mylib
//   src/engine.cpp
//   src/parser.cpp
// )
// add_library(MyLib::mylib ALIAS mylib)  # namespace alias
//
// target_include_directories(mylib
//   PUBLIC
//     $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include>
//     $<INSTALL_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR}>
// )
//
// # Install library binary
// install(TARGETS mylib
//   EXPORT MyLibTargets
//   LIBRARY DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
//   ARCHIVE DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
//   RUNTIME DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_BINDIR}
// )
//
// # Install headers
// install(DIRECTORY include/mylib
//   DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR}
// )
//
// # Generate and install CMake config files
// install(EXPORT MyLibTargets
//   FILE MyLibTargets.cmake
//   NAMESPACE MyLib::
//   DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
// )
//
// configure_package_config_file(
//   cmake/MyLibConfig.cmake.in
//   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfig.cmake
//   INSTALL_DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
// )
//
// write_basic_package_version_file(
//   MyLibConfigVersion.cmake
//   VERSION ${PROJECT_VERSION}
//   COMPATIBILITY SameMajorVersion
// )
//
// install(FILES
//   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfig.cmake
//   ${CMAKE_CURRENT_BINARY_DIR}/MyLibConfigVersion.cmake
//   DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}/cmake/MyLib
// )

// === include/mylib/engine.h ===
#pragma once
#include <string>

namespace mylib {
class Engine {
public:
    Engine();
    std::string process(const std::string& input);
};
}

// === Consumer CMakeLists.txt ===
// find_package(MyLib 1.0 REQUIRED)
// target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE MyLib::mylib)

// Install: cmake --install build/release --prefix /usr/local
// Then any CMake project can find_package(MyLib)
```

instruction: How should I organize a larger C++ project with multiple libraries and executables using CMake?
output:
<think>
For larger projects, structure into subdirectories with each library as a separate CMake target. The root CMakeLists.txt orchestrates dependencies between them. Each subdirectory is self-contained with its own CMakeLists.txt.

The recommended layout: src/ for production code (subdivided into libraries), apps/ for executables, tests/ for test code, include/ for public headers (if building an installable library), third_party/ or using FetchContent for external deps.

Use OBJECT libraries for internal code that should not be a separate .a/.so but needs to share code between executables. Use INTERFACE libraries for header-only components. Use STATIC/SHARED for actual library artifacts.

The dependency chain should be explicit: target_link_libraries propagates include paths and compile flags. If lib_network depends on lib_core, and app_server depends on lib_network, then app_server automatically gets lib_core's headers and flags through transitive dependencies.
</think>

```cpp
// === Project layout ===
// myproject/
// +-- CMakeLists.txt         (root)
// +-- CMakePresets.json
// +-- src/
// |   +-- core/              (core library)
// |   |   +-- CMakeLists.txt
// |   |   +-- include/core/
// |   |   |   +-- types.h
// |   |   +-- types.cpp
// |   +-- network/           (network library, depends on core)
// |   |   +-- CMakeLists.txt
// |   |   +-- include/network/
// |   |   |   +-- client.h
// |   |   +-- client.cpp
// |   +-- storage/           (storage library, depends on core)
// |       +-- CMakeLists.txt
// |       +-- include/storage/
// |       +-- database.cpp
// +-- apps/
// |   +-- server/
// |   |   +-- CMakeLists.txt
// |   |   +-- main.cpp
// |   +-- cli/
// |       +-- CMakeLists.txt
// |       +-- main.cpp
// +-- tests/
//     +-- CMakeLists.txt
//     +-- core_test.cpp
//     +-- network_test.cpp

// === src/core/CMakeLists.txt ===
// add_library(core
//   types.cpp
// )
// target_include_directories(core
//   PUBLIC ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include
// )

// === src/network/CMakeLists.txt ===
// add_library(network
//   client.cpp
// )
// target_include_directories(network
//   PUBLIC ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include
// )
// target_link_libraries(network PUBLIC core)  # transitive dep

// === apps/server/CMakeLists.txt ===
// add_executable(server main.cpp)
// target_link_libraries(server PRIVATE network storage)
// # Gets core headers transitively through network

// === Root CMakeLists.txt ===
// cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.25)
// project(myproject VERSION 1.0.0 LANGUAGES CXX)
// set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
// add_subdirectory(src/core)
// add_subdirectory(src/network)
// add_subdirectory(src/storage)
// add_subdirectory(apps/server)
// add_subdirectory(apps/cli)
// if(BUILD_TESTING)
//   add_subdirectory(tests)
// endif()

#include <core/types.h>
#include <network/client.h>
// Includes work because CMake propagates include paths

int main() {
    // server app has access to core + network + storage
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: How do I use FetchContent vs find_package vs vcpkg for C++ dependency management?
output:
<think>
Three main approaches for C++ dependencies, each with trade-offs:

1. FetchContent: Downloads and builds deps at configure time. Best for small-to-medium deps, header-only libraries, and when you want reproducible builds pinned to specific commits. Downside: increases configure time, rebuilds deps from source.

2. find_package: Finds pre-installed libraries on the system. Best for large deps you do not want to rebuild (Boost, Qt, OpenCV). Requires the dependency to be installed first (via system package manager or manual install).

3. vcpkg: Microsoft's C++ package manager. Best of both worlds: installs pre-built binaries, integrates with CMake via toolchain file. Supports manifest mode (vcpkg.json) for reproducible dependency sets.

My recommendation: use vcpkg for projects with many dependencies (it handles the build/install automatically). Use FetchContent for 1-3 small deps or for pinning exact versions. Use find_package for system-installed large frameworks.
</think>

```cpp
// === Method 1: FetchContent (download + build) ===
// CMakeLists.txt:
// include(FetchContent)
// FetchContent_Declare(
//   nlohmann_json
//   GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/nlohmann/json.git
//   GIT_TAG v3.11.3
// )
// FetchContent_Declare(
//   spdlog
//   GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/gabime/spdlog.git
//   GIT_TAG v1.13.0
// )
// FetchContent_MakeAvailable(nlohmann_json spdlog)
// target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE nlohmann_json::nlohmann_json spdlog::spdlog)

// === Method 2: find_package (pre-installed) ===
// CMakeLists.txt:
// find_package(Boost 1.84 REQUIRED COMPONENTS filesystem system)
// find_package(OpenSSL REQUIRED)
// target_link_libraries(myapp
//   PRIVATE Boost::filesystem Boost::system OpenSSL::SSL
// )

// === Method 3: vcpkg (package manager) ===
// vcpkg.json (manifest):
// {
//   "name": "myapp",
//   "version": "1.0.0",
//   "dependencies": [
//     "fmt",
//     "spdlog",
//     "nlohmann-json",
//     { "name": "boost-filesystem", "version>=": "1.84.0" },
//     { "name": "openssl", "platform": "linux" }
//   ]
// }
//
// CMakePresets.json:
// { "configurePresets": [{
//   "name": "vcpkg",
//   "toolchainFile": "$env{VCPKG_ROOT}/scripts/buildsystems/vcpkg.cmake"
// }]}

// With vcpkg, find_package works automatically:
// find_package(fmt CONFIG REQUIRED)
// find_package(spdlog CONFIG REQUIRED)
// target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE fmt::fmt spdlog::spdlog)

#include <fmt/core.h>
#include <spdlog/spdlog.h>
#include <nlohmann/json.hpp>

int main() {
    spdlog::info("Starting app");
    nlohmann::json config = {{"key", "value"}};
    fmt::print("Config: {}\n", config.dump());
    return 0;
}

// Build with vcpkg:
// cmake --preset vcpkg
// cmake --build build/vcpkg
```
